phrasalVerbs.definition: To register your arrival at a hotel, airport, or event.
examples: We need to check in at the hotel by 3 PM.
Please check in at the airport at least two hours before your flight.
You can check in online to save time.
We set off early to avoid the traffic.
What time are you setting off tomorrow morning?
They set off on their road trip at sunrise.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To collect someone or something from a place; also to learn something informally.
examples: Can you pick me up from the airport at 6?
She picked up a few words of French during her trip.
I'll pick up some souvenirs for the family.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To take someone or something to a place and leave them or it there.
examples: Can you drop me off near the train station?
I'll drop off your luggage at the hotel.
She dropped off the hire car before catching her flight.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To travel from place to place, especially in a new city.
examples: It's easy to get around the city by metro.
How did you get around without a car?
The best way to get around is by bike.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To explore a place by walking and observing.
examples: We spent the morning looking around the old town.
Do you want to look around the museum first?
I love to look around local markets when I travel.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ at the airport three hours before the flight.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Check in' means to register your arrival at an airport or hotel.

fillInExercises.sentence: We ___ off at 5 AM to beat the morning traffic.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Set off' (past: set off) means to start a journey.

fillInExercises.sentence: Can you ___ me from the station? My train arrives at 8.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pick up' means to collect someone from a location.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ off her rental car at the airport before boarding.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Drop off' (past: dropped off) means to leave something or someone somewhere.

fillInExercises.sentence: It's easy to ___ Rome on foot — everything is close together.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get around' means to travel from place to place in a city.

fillInExercises.sentence: We spent two hours ___ around the old quarter before lunch.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Look around' means to explore a place by walking and observing.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ some Spanish while backpacking through South America.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pick up' also means to learn something informally.

fillInExercises.sentence: What time do you plan to ___ off on your road trip?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Set off' means to begin a journey.

lines.speaker: Raj
lines.text: I'm landing soon. Can you [pick up] me from Terminal 2?

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: Of course! Have you [check in] your luggage already?

lines.speaker: Raj
lines.text: Yes — I had to [drop off] my bag at the desk though.

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: OK. I'll [set off] as soon as I finish this call.

lines.speaker: Raj
lines.text: I can't wait to [look around] the city — it's been years!

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: I'll show you how to [get around] by tram. It's really easy.

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: I want to [set off] really early on Saturday to avoid the crowds.

lines.speaker: David
lines.text: Good idea. We can [check in] to the hotel by noon if we leave at 7.

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: Then we can [look around] the market district in the afternoon.

lines.speaker: David
lines.text: It's easy to [get around] by bike there — I already checked.

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: Great. I'll [drop off] the keys to my neighbour before we go.

lines.speaker: David
lines.text: And I'll [pick up] some snacks for the journey. It's a 3-hour drive.
